content.text: is an object that represents an asynchronous operation and its eventual result. Before Promises, async code relied on deeply nested callbacks — the notorious "callback hell." Promises give asynchronous operations a predictable, chainable interface that makes the flow of data and errors easy to follow.
Every Promise exists in exactly one of three states, and transitions are permanent — once a Promise settles (fulfills or rejects), its state and value never change:
— the operation has not completed yet. This is the initial state.
— the operation completed successfully. The Promise holds a value.
— the operation failed. The Promise holds a reason (usually an

attrs.alt: Promise lifecycle diagram: Pending transitions to Fulfilled via resolve or Rejected via reject. Fulfilled calls .then(), Rejected calls .catch(). .finally() always runs.

Library APIs (React Query, SWR, Redux Thunk) return or consume Promises directly, so you need to read Promise-based code.
Because Promises are asynchronous, tests must wait for them before making assertions. Vitest and Jest both support returning a Promise from a test function — the test runner waits for it to settle:
Two static helpers create already-settled Promises — useful in tests and for returning early from async functions:
